question:
Call a subset $S$ of $\{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ \emph{mediocre} if it has the following property:
Whenever $a$ and $b$ are elements of $S$ whose average is an integer, that average is also
an element of $S$. Let $A(n)$ be the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,2,\dots,n\}$.
[For instance, every subset of $\{1,2,3\}$ except $\{1,3\}$ is mediocre, so $A(3) =7$.]
Find all positive integers $n$ such that $A(n+2) - 2A(n+1) + A(n) = 1$.
solution:
The answer is $n=2^k-1$ for some integer $k\geq 1$.
There is a bijection between mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,n\}$ and
mediocre subsets of $\{2,\ldots,n+1\}$ given by adding $1$ to each
element of the subset; thus $A(n+1)-A(n)$ is the number of mediocre
subsets of $\{1,\ldots,n+1\}$ that contain $1$. It follows that
$A(n+2)-2A(n+1)+A_n = (A(n+2)-A(n+1))-(A(n+1)-A(n))$ is the difference
between the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,n+2\}$ containing
$1$ and the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,n+1\}$ containing
$1$. This difference is precisely the number of mediocre subsets of
$\{1,\ldots,n+2\}$ containing both $1$ and $n+2$, which we term
``mediocre subsets containing the endpoints.'' Since $\{1,\ldots,n+2\}$
itself is a mediocre subset of itself containing the endpoints, it
suffices to prove that this is the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,n+2\}$ containing the endpoints if and only if $n=2^k-1$ for
some $k$.

If $n$ is not of the form $2^k-1$, then we can write $n+1 = 2^a b$ for
odd $b>1$. In this case, the set $\{1+m b \, | \, 0 \leq m \leq 2^a\}$
is a mediocre subset of $\{1,\ldots,n+2\}$ containing the endpoints: the
average of $1+m_1 b$ and $1+m_2 b$, namely $1+\frac{m_1+m_2}{2} b$, is
an integer if and only if $m_1+m_2$ is even, in which case this average
lies in the set.

It remains to show that if $n=2^k-1$, then the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,n+2\}$ containing the endpoints is itself. This is readily
seen by induction on $k$. For $k=1$, the statement is obvious. For
general $k$, any mediocre subset $S$ of $\{1,\ldots,n+2=2^k+1\}$
containing $1$ and $2^k+1$ must also contain their average, $2^{k-1}+1$.
By the induction assumption, the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,2^{k-1}+1\}$ containing the endpoints is itself, and so $S$
must contain all integers between $1$ and $2^{k-1}+1$. Similarly, a
mediocre subset of $\{2^{k-1}+1,\ldots,2^k+1\}$ containing the endpoints
gives a mediocre subset of $\{1,\ldots,2^{k-1}+1\}$ containing the
endpoints by subtracting $2^{k-1}$ from each element. By the induction
assumption again, it follows that $S$ must contain all integers between
$2^{k-1}+1$ and $2^k+1$. Thus $S = \{1,\ldots,2^k+1\}$ and the induction
is complete.

\textbf{Remark.} One can also proceed by checking that a nonempty subset of
$\{1,\dots,n\}$ is mediocre if and only if it is an arithmetic progression
with odd common difference. Given this fact, the number of
mediocre subsets of $\{1,\dots,n+2\}$ containing the endpoints is seen to be
the number of odd factors of $n+1$, from which the desired result is evident.
(The sequence $A(n)$ appears as sequence A124197 in the Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences.)

descriptive_long.question:
Call a subset $subset$ of $\{1, 2, \dots, setsize\}$ \emph{mediocre} if it has the following property:
Whenever $alphaidx$ and $betaidx$ are elements of $subset$ whose average is an integer, that average is also
an element of $subset$. Let $setcount(setsize)$ be the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,2,\dots,setsize\}$.
[For instance, every subset of $\{1,2,3\}$ except $\{1,3\}$ is mediocre, so $setcount(3) =7$.]
Find all positive integers $setsize$ such that $setcount(setsize+2) - 2setcount(setsize+1) + setcount(setsize) = 1$.
descriptive_long.solution:
The answer is $setsize=2^{poweridx}-1$ for some integer $poweridx\geq 1$.
There is a bijection between mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,setsize\}$ and
mediocre subsets of $\{2,\ldots,setsize+1\}$ given by adding $1$ to each
element of the subset; thus $setcount(setsize+1)-setcount(setsize)$ is the number of mediocre
subsets of $\{1,\ldots,setsize+1\}$ that contain $1$. It follows that
$setcount(setsize+2)-2setcount(setsize+1)+setcountn = (setcount(setsize+2)-setcount(setsize+1))-(setcount(setsize+1)-setcount(setsize))$ is the difference
between the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,setsize+2\}$ containing
$1$ and the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,setsize+1\}$ containing
$1$. This difference is precisely the number of mediocre subsets of
$\{1,\ldots,setsize+2\}$ containing both $1$ and $setsize+2$, which we term
``mediocre subsets containing the endpoints.'' Since $\{1,\ldots,setsize+2\}$
itself is a mediocre subset of itself containing the endpoints, it
suffices to prove that this is the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,setsize+2\}$ containing the endpoints if and only if $setsize=2^{poweridx}-1$ for
some $poweridx$.

If $setsize$ is not of the form $2^{poweridx}-1$, then we can write $setsize+1 = 2^{alphaidx} \, betaidx$ for
odd $betaidx>1$. In this case, the set $\{1+indexm \, betaidx \, | \, 0 \leq indexm \leq 2^{alphaidx}\}$
is a mediocre subset of $\{1,\ldots,setsize+2\}$ containing the endpoints: the
average of $1+indexone \, betaidx$ and $1+indextwo \, betaidx$, namely $1+\frac{indexone+indextwo}{2} \, betaidx$, is
an integer if and only if $indexone+indextwo$ is even, in which case this average
lies in the set.

It remains to show that if $setsize=2^{poweridx}-1$, then the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,setsize+2\}$ containing the endpoints is itself. This is readily
seen by induction on $poweridx$. For $poweridx=1$, the statement is obvious. For
general $poweridx$, any mediocre subset $subset$ of $\{1,\ldots,setsize+2=2^{poweridx}+1\}$
containing $1$ and $2^{poweridx}+1$ must also contain their average, $2^{poweridx-1}+1$.
By the induction assumption, the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,2^{poweridx-1}+1\}$ containing the endpoints is itself, and so $subset$
must contain all integers between $1$ and $2^{poweridx-1}+1$. Similarly, a
mediocre subset of $\{2^{poweridx-1}+1,\ldots,2^{poweridx}+1\}$ containing the endpoints
gives a mediocre subset of $\{1,\ldots,2^{poweridx-1}+1\}$ containing the
endpoints by subtracting $2^{poweridx-1}$ from each element. By the induction
assumption again, it follows that $subset$ must contain all integers between
$2^{poweridx-1}+1$ and $2^{poweridx}+1$. Thus $subset = \{1,\ldots,2^{poweridx}+1\}$ and the induction
is complete.

\textbf{Remark.} One can also proceed by checking that a nonempty subset of
$\{1,\dots,setsize\}$ is mediocre if and only if it is an arithmetic progression
with odd common difference. Given this fact, the number of
mediocre subsets of $\{1,\dots,setsize+2\}$ containing the endpoints is seen to be
the number of odd factors of $setsize+1$, from which the desired result is evident.
(The sequence $setcount(setsize)$ appears as sequence A124197 in the Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences.)

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Call a subset $raincloud$ of $\{1, 2, \dots, starfruit\}$ \emph{mediocre} if it has the following property:
Whenever $honeycomb$ and $drumstick$ are elements of $raincloud$ whose average is an integer, that average is also
an element of $raincloud$. Let $lighthouse(starfruit)$ be the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,2,\dots,starfruit\}$.
[For instance, every subset of $\{1,2,3\}$ except $\{1,3\}$ is mediocre, so $lighthouse(3) =7$.]
Find all positive integers $starfruit$ such that $lighthouse(starfruit+2) - 2lighthouse(starfruit+1) + lighthouse(starfruit) = 1$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The answer is $starfruit=2^{paintbrush}-1$ for some integer $paintbrush\geq 1$.
There is a bijection between mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,starfruit\}$ and
mediocre subsets of $\{2,\ldots,starfruit+1\}$ given by adding $1$ to each
element of the subset; thus $lighthouse(starfruit+1)-lighthouse(starfruit)$ is the number of mediocre
subsets of $\{1,\ldots,starfruit+1\}$ that contain $1$. It follows that
$lighthouse(starfruit+2)-2lighthouse(starfruit+1)+seashore = (lighthouse(starfruit+2)-lighthouse(starfruit+1))-(lighthouse(starfruit+1)-lighthouse(starfruit))$ is the difference
between the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,starfruit+2\}$ containing
$1$ and the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,starfruit+1\}$ containing
$1$. This difference is precisely the number of mediocre subsets of
$\{1,\ldots,starfruit+2\}$ containing both $1$ and $starfruit+2$, which we term
``mediocre subsets containing the endpoints.'' Since $\{1,\ldots,starfruit+2\}$
itself is a mediocre subset of itself containing the endpoints, it
suffices to prove that this is the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,starfruit+2\}$ containing the endpoints if and only if $starfruit=2^{paintbrush}-1$ for
some $paintbrush$.

If $starfruit$ is not of the form $2^{paintbrush}-1$, then we can write $starfruit+1 = 2^{honeycomb} drumstick$ for
odd $drumstick>1$. In this case, the set $\{1+blackbird drumstick \, | \, 0 \leq blackbird \leq 2^{honeycomb}\}$
is a mediocre subset of $\{1,\ldots,starfruit+2\}$ containing the endpoints: the
average of $1+sledgehammer drumstick$ and $1+peppercorn drumstick$, namely $1+\frac{sledgehammer+peppercorn}{2} drumstick$, is
an integer if and only if $sledgehammer+peppercorn$ is even, in which case this average
lies in the set.

It remains to show that if $starfruit=2^{paintbrush}-1$, then the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,starfruit+2\}$ containing the endpoints is itself. This is readily
seen by induction on $paintbrush$. For $paintbrush=1$, the statement is obvious. For
general $paintbrush$, any mediocre subset $raincloud$ of $\{1,\ldots,starfruit+2=2^{paintbrush}+1\}$
containing $1$ and $2^{paintbrush}+1$ must also contain their average, $2^{paintbrush-1}+1$.
By the induction assumption, the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,2^{paintbrush-1}+1\}$ containing the endpoints is itself, and so $raincloud$
must contain all integers between $1$ and $2^{paintbrush-1}+1$. Similarly, a
mediocre subset of $\{2^{paintbrush-1}+1,\ldots,2^{paintbrush}+1\}$ containing the endpoints
gives a mediocre subset of $\{1,\ldots,2^{paintbrush-1}+1\}$ containing the
endpoints by subtracting $2^{paintbrush-1}$ from each element. By the induction
assumption again, it follows that $raincloud$ must contain all integers between
$2^{paintbrush-1}+1$ and $2^{paintbrush}+1$. Thus $raincloud = \{1,\ldots,2^{paintbrush}+1\}$ and the induction
is complete.

\textbf{Remark.} One can also proceed by checking that a nonempty subset of
$\{1,\dots,starfruit\}$ is mediocre if and only if it is an arithmetic progression
with odd common difference. Given this fact, the number of
mediocre subsets of $\{1,\dots,starfruit+2\}$ containing the endpoints is seen to be
the number of odd factors of $starfruit+1$, from which the desired result is evident.
(The sequence $lighthouse(starfruit)$ appears as sequence A124197 in the Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences.)

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Call a subset $emptiness$ of $\{1, 2, \dots, infinitum\}$ \emph{mediocre} if it has the following property:
Whenever $totality$ and $evenness$ are elements of $emptiness$ whose average is an integer, that average is also
an element of $emptiness$. Let $scarcity(infinitum)$ be the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,2,\dots,infinitum\}$.
[For instance, every subset of $\{1,2,3\}$ except $\{1,3\}$ is mediocre, so $scarcity(3) =7$.]
Find all positive integers $infinitum$ such that $scarcity(infinitum+2) - 2scarcity(infinitum+1) + scarcity(infinitum) = 1$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The answer is $infinitum=2^{shrinkage}-1$ for some integer $shrinkage\geq 1$.
There is a bijection between mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,infinitum\}$ and
mediocre subsets of $\{2,\ldots,infinitum+1\}$ given by adding $1$ to each
element of the subset; thus $scarcity(infinitum+1)-scarcity(infinitum)$ is the number of mediocre
subsets of $\{1,\ldots,infinitum+1\}$ that contain $1$. It follows that
$scarcity(infinitum+2)-2scarcity(infinitum+1)+scarcityindex = (scarcity(infinitum+2)-scarcity(infinitum+1))-(scarcity(infinitum+1)-scarcity(infinitum))$ is the difference
between the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,infinitum+2\}$ containing
$1$ and the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,infinitum+1\}$ containing
$1$. This difference is precisely the number of mediocre subsets of
$\{1,\ldots,infinitum+2\}$ containing both $1$ and $infinitum+2$, which we term
``mediocre subsets containing the endpoints.'' Since $\{1,\ldots,infinitum+2\}$
itself is a mediocre subset of itself containing the endpoints, it
suffices to prove that this is the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,infinitum+2\}$ containing the endpoints if and only if $infinitum=2^{shrinkage}-1$ for
some $shrinkage$.

If $infinitum$ is not of the form $2^{shrinkage}-1$, then we can write $infinitum+1 = 2^{totality} evenness$ for
odd $evenness>1$. In this case, the set $\{1+stillness\, evenness \, | \, 0 \leq stillness \leq 2^{totality}\}$
is a mediocre subset of $\{1,\ldots,infinitum+2\}$ containing the endpoints: the
average of $1+stillnessone\, evenness$ and $1+stillnesstwo\, evenness$, namely $1+\frac{stillnessone+stillnesstwo}{2}\, evenness$, is
an integer if and only if $stillnessone+stillnesstwo$ is even, in which case this average
lies in the set.

It remains to show that if $infinitum=2^{shrinkage}-1$, then the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,infinitum+2\}$ containing the endpoints is itself. This is readily
seen by induction on $shrinkage$. For $shrinkage=1$, the statement is obvious. For
general $shrinkage$, any mediocre subset $emptiness$ of $\{1,\ldots,infinitum+2=2^{shrinkage}+1\}$
containing $1$ and $2^{shrinkage}+1$ must also contain their average, $2^{shrinkage-1}+1$.
By the induction assumption, the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,2^{shrinkage-1}+1\}$ containing the endpoints is itself, and so $emptiness$
must contain all integers between $1$ and $2^{shrinkage-1}+1$. Similarly, a
mediocre subset of $\{2^{shrinkage-1}+1,\ldots,2^{shrinkage}+1\}$ containing the endpoints
gives a mediocre subset of $\{1,\ldots,2^{shrinkage-1}+1\}$ containing the
endpoints by subtracting $2^{shrinkage-1}$ from each element. By the induction
assumption again, it follows that $emptiness$ must contain all integers between
$2^{shrinkage-1}+1$ and $2^{shrinkage}+1$. Thus $emptiness = \{1,\ldots,2^{shrinkage}+1\}$ and the induction
is complete.

\textbf{Remark.} One can also proceed by checking that a nonempty subset of
$\{1,\dots,infinitum\}$ is mediocre if and only if it is an arithmetic progression
with odd common difference. Given this fact, the number of
mediocre subsets of $\{1,\dots,infinitum+2\}$ containing the endpoints is seen to be
the number of odd factors of $infinitum+1$, from which the desired result is evident.
(The sequence $scarcity(infinitum)$ appears as sequence A124197 in the Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences.)

garbled_string.question:
Call a subset $qxvjmrlp$ of $\{1, 2, \dots, flkzoqeir\}$ \emph{mediocre} if it has the following property:
Whenever $ysrvkmeh$ and $cndqlohz$ are elements of $qxvjmrlp$ whose average is an integer, that average is also
an element of $qxvjmrlp$. Let $rldvwxje(flkzoqeir)$ be the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,2,\dots,flkzoqeir\}$.
[For instance, every subset of $\{1,2,3\}$ except $\{1,3\}$ is mediocre, so $rldvwxje(3) =7$.]
Find all positive integers $flkzoqeir$ such that $rldvwxje(flkzoqeir+2) - 2rldvwxje(flkzoqeir+1) + rldvwxje(flkzoqeir) = 1$.
garbled_string.solution:
The answer is $flkzoqeir=2^{xugfdpav}-1$ for some integer $xugfdpav\geq 1$.
There is a bijection between mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,flkzoqeir\}$ and
mediocre subsets of $\{2,\ldots,flkzoqeir+1\}$ given by adding $1$ to each
element of the subset; thus $rldvwxje(flkzoqeir+1)-rldvwxje(flkzoqeir)$ is the number of mediocre
subsets of $\{1,\ldots,flkzoqeir+1\}$ that contain $1$. It follows that
$rldvwxje(flkzoqeir+2)-2rldvwxje(flkzoqeir+1)+zmbghwso = (rldvwxje(flkzoqeir+2)-rldvwxje(flkzoqeir+1))-(rldvwxje(flkzoqeir+1)-rldvwxje(flkzoqeir))$ is the difference
between the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,flkzoqeir+2\}$ containing
$1$ and the number of mediocre subsets of $\{1,\ldots,flkzoqeir+1\}$ containing
$1$. This difference is precisely the number of mediocre subsets of
$\{1,\ldots,flkzoqeir+2\}$ containing both $1$ and $flkzoqeir+2$, which we term
``mediocre subsets containing the endpoints.'' Since $\{1,\ldots,flkzoqeir+2\}$
itself is a mediocre subset of itself containing the endpoints, it
suffices to prove that this is the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,flkzoqeir+2\}$ containing the endpoints if and only if $flkzoqeir=2^{xugfdpav}-1$ for
some $xugfdpav$.

If $flkzoqeir$ is not of the form $2^{xugfdpav}-1$, then we can write $flkzoqeir+1 = 2^{ysrvkmeh} cndqlohz$ for
odd $cndqlohz>1$. In this case, the set $\{1+hsjqvnto\,cndqlohz \, | \, 0 \leq hsjqvnto \leq 2^{ysrvkmeh}\}$
is a mediocre subset of $\{1,\ldots,flkzoqeir+2\}$ containing the endpoints: the
average of $1+wprkebal\,cndqlohz$ and $1+znctigoh\,cndqlohz$, namely $1+\frac{wprkebal+znctigoh}{2}\,cndqlohz$, is
an integer if and only if $wprkebal+znctigoh$ is even, in which case this average
lies in the set.

It remains to show that if $flkzoqeir=2^{xugfdpav}-1$, then the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,flkzoqeir+2\}$ containing the endpoints is itself. This is readily
seen by induction on $xugfdpav$. For $xugfdpav=1$, the statement is obvious. For
general $xugfdpav$, any mediocre subset $qxvjmrlp$ of $\{1,\ldots,flkzoqeir+2=2^{xugfdpav}+1\}$
containing $1$ and $2^{xugfdpav}+1$ must also contain their average, $2^{xugfdpav-1}+1$.
By the induction assumption, the only mediocre subset of
$\{1,\ldots,2^{xugfdpav-1}+1\}$ containing the endpoints is itself, and so $qxvjmrlp$
must contain all integers between $1$ and $2^{xugfdpav-1}+1$. Similarly, a
mediocre subset of $\{2^{xugfdpav-1}+1,\ldots,2^{xugfdpav}+1\}$ containing the endpoints
gives a mediocre subset of $\{1,\ldots,2^{xugfdpav-1}+1\}$ containing the endpoints by subtracting $2^{xugfdpav-1}$ from each element. By the induction
assumption again, it follows that $qxvjmrlp$ must contain all integers between
$2^{xugfdpav-1}+1$ and $2^{xugfdpav}+1$. Thus $qxvjmrlp = \{1,\ldots,2^{xugfdpav}+1\}$ and the induction
is complete.

\textbf{Remark.} One can also proceed by checking that a nonempty subset of
$\{1,\dots,flkzoqeir\}$ is mediocre if and only if it is an arithmetic progression
with odd common difference. Given this fact, the number of
mediocre subsets of $\{1,\dots,flkzoqeir+2\}$ containing the endpoints is seen to be
the number of odd factors of $flkzoqeir+1$, from which the desired result is evident.
(The sequence $rldvwxje(flkzoqeir)$ appears as sequence A124197 in the Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences.)

kernel_variant.question:
Call a subset $S$ of $\{3,4,\dots ,m\}$ mediocre if, whenever $a,b\in S$ have integer average, that average also lies in $S$.  For $m\ge 3$ let \[B(m)=\#\{\text{mediocre subsets of }\{3,4,\dots ,m\}\}.\]
Determine all positive integers $n$ such that
\[
B(n+3)-2B(n+2)+B(n+1)=1.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  For m\geq 3 let B(m) count the mediocre subsets of {3,4,\ldots ,m}.  By the usual ``shift by 1'' bijection one shows
  B(m+1)-B(m)=#{mediocre S\subseteq {3,\ldots ,m+1}:3\in S}.
Indeed, sending each mediocre subset of {3,\ldots ,m} to its image under x\mapsto x+1 gives all mediocre subsets of {4,\ldots ,m+1}, so the new ones are exactly those containing the new element 3.

Step 2.  Hence
  B(m+3)-2B(m+2)+B(m+1)
  =(B(m+3)-B(m+2))-(B(m+2)-B(m+1))
  =#{S\subseteq {3,\ldots ,m+3}:3\in S}-#{S\subseteq {3,\ldots ,m+2}:3\in S}
  =#{mediocre S\subseteq {3,\ldots ,m+3}:3\in S and m+3\in S}.

Step 3.  It is standard that a nonempty subset of consecutive integers is mediocre if and only if it is an arithmetic progression whose common difference is odd.  Therefore any mediocre S\subseteq {3,\ldots ,m+3} with 3,m+3\in S must be
  S={3,3+d,3+2d,\ldots ,3+kd=m+3}
with d>0 odd and kd=m.  Conversely each odd divisor d of m gives exactly one such S.  Thus
  B(m+3)-2B(m+2)+B(m+1)=d_-odd(m),
where d_-odd(m) is the number of odd divisors of m.

Step 4.  The equation
  B(n+3)-2B(n+2)+B(n+1)=1
becomes d_-odd(n)=1, which holds exactly when n has no odd divisor >1, i.e. n is a power of 2.  Since B(n+1) is only defined for n+1\geq 3 we require n\geq 2.  Hence the solutions are exactly
  n=2,4,8,16,\ldots 
all positive powers of 2 (\geq 2).  This completes the solution.

core_steps: First-difference trick: A(n+1)−A(n) equals the number of mediocre subsets of {1,…,n+1} that contain the left endpoint (via +1 shift bijection).
Second-difference trick: A(n+2)−2A(n+1)+A(n) counts mediocre subsets of {1,…,n+2} that contain both endpoints 1 and n+2.
Construction when n+1 has an odd factor >1: an arithmetic progression 1,1+b,1+2b,…,1+2^a b provides a second endpoint-containing mediocre set, so the second difference exceeds 1.
Uniqueness when n+1 is a power of two: inductively show that any endpoint-containing mediocre set must successively fill the whole interval, hence the full set is the only possibility.
Therefore the equation equals 1 exactly when n = 2^k − 1 (k ≥ 1).

slot1.description: Actual label of the left endpoint of the ambient interval; any constant shift of all elements preserves the argument.

slot2.description: Size of the forward step in the finite-difference A(n+step)−2A(n+step−1)+A(n+step−2); step=2 here but any fixed step ≥2 keeps the same counting logic (it will then refer to sets containing both endpoints distance ‘step’ apart).
